name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَىٰ قَوْمِهِۦ فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عَامًا فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلطُّوفَانُ وَهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
Certainly We sent Noah to his people, and he remained with them for a thousand-less-fifty years. Then the flood overtook them while they were wrongdoers. (14)
```

```arabic
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَأَصْحَـٰبَ ٱلسَّفِينَةِ وَجَعَلْنَـٰهَآ ءَايَةً لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٥)
```

```english
Then We delivered him and those who were in the Ark, and made it a sign for all the nations. (15)
```

```arabic
وَإِبْرَٰهِيمَ إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ ۖ ذَٰلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ (١٦)
```

```english
And Abraham, when he said to his people, ‘Worship Allah and be wary of Him. That is better for you, should you know. (16)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْثَـٰنًا وَتَخْلُقُونَ إِفْكًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لَكُمْ رِزْقًا فَٱبْتَغُوا۟ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلرِّزْقَ وَٱعْبُدُوهُ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لَهُۥٓ ۖ إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
What you worship instead of Allah are mere idols, and you invent a lie. Indeed those whom you worship besides Allah have no control over your provision. So seek all [your] provision from Allah, and worship Him and thank Him, and to Him you shall be brought back.’ (17)
```

```arabic
وَإِن تُكَذِّبُوا۟ فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أُمَمٌ مِّن قَبْلِكُمْ ۖ وَمَا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (١٨)
```

```english
If you impugn [the Apostle’s teaching], then [other] nations have impugned [likewise] before you, and the Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms. (18)
```

```arabic
أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ كَيْفَ يُبْدِئُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (١٩)
```

```english
Have they not regarded how Allah originates the creation? Then He will bring it back. That is indeed easy for Allah. (19)
```

```arabic
قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ بَدَأَ ٱلْخَلْقَ ۚ ثُمَّ ٱللَّهُ يُنشِئُ ٱلنَّشْأَةَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٢٠)
```

```english
Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how He has originated the creation.’ Then Allah will bring about the genesis of the Hereafter. Indeed Allah has power over all things. (20)
```

```arabic
يُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَرْحَمُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ تُقْلَبُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
He will punish whomever He wishes and have mercy on whomever He wishes, and to Him you will be returned. (21)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ ۖ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٢٢)
```

```english
You cannot frustrate Him on the earth or in the sky, nor do you have besides Allah any guardian or helper. (22)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِقَآئِهِۦٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَئِسُوا۟ مِن رَّحْمَتِى وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢٣)
```

```english
Those who deny the signs of Allah and the encounter with Him—they have despaired of My mercy, and for such there is a painful punishment. (23)
```

```arabic
فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱقْتُلُوهُ أَوْ حَرِّقُوهُ فَأَنجَىٰهُ ٱللَّهُ مِنَ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٢٤)
```

```english
But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Kill him, or burn him.’ Then Allah delivered him from the fire. There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (24)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ إِنَّمَا ٱتَّخَذْتُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ أَوْثَـٰنًا مَّوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ ثُمَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يَكْفُرُ بَعْضُكُم بِبَعْضٍ وَيَلْعَنُ بَعْضُكُم بَعْضًا وَمَأْوَىٰكُمُ ٱلنَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٢٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘You have taken idols [for worship] besides Allah for the sake of [mutual] affection amongst yourselves in the life of the world. Then on the Day of Resurrection you will disown one another and curse one another, and the Fire will be your abode, and you will not have any helpers.’ (25)
```

```arabic
۞ فَـَٔامَنَ لَهُۥ لُوطٌ ۘ وَقَالَ إِنِّى مُهَاجِرٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٦)
```

```english
Thereupon Lot believed in him, and he said, ‘Indeed I am migrating toward my Lord. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (26)
```

```arabic
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ذُرِّيَّتِهِ ٱلنُّبُوَّةَ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ أَجْرَهُۥ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَإِنَّهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٢٧)
```

```english
And We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and We ordained among his descendants prophethood and the Book, and We gave him his reward in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous. (27)
```

```arabic
وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُم بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٨)
```

```english
And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘You indeed commit an indecency none in the world has ever committed before you! (28)
```

```arabic
أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلرِّجَالَ وَتَقْطَعُونَ ٱلسَّبِيلَ وَتَأْتُونَ فِى نَادِيكُمُ ٱلْمُنكَرَ ۖ فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ ٱئْتِنَا بِعَذَابِ ٱللَّهِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٢٩)
```

```english
What! Do you come to men, and cut off the way, and commit outrages in your gatherings?’ But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Bring down us Allah’s punishment, if you are truthful.’ (29)
```

```arabic
قَالَ رَبِّ ٱنصُرْنِى عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٣٠)
```

```english
He said, ‘My Lord! Help me against this corruptive lot.’ (30)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَّا جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ بِٱلْبُشْرَىٰ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوٓا۟ أَهْلِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ۖ إِنَّ أَهْلَهَا كَانُوا۟ ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
And when Our messengers came to Abraham with the good news, they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town. Its people are indeed wrongdoers.’ (31)
```

```arabic
قَالَ إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا ۚ قَالُوا۟ نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن فِيهَا ۖ لَنُنَجِّيَنَّهُۥ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٣٢)
```

```english
He said, ‘Lot is in it.’ They said, ‘We know better those who are in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind.’ (32)
```

```arabic
وَلَمَّآ أَن جَآءَتْ رُسُلُنَا لُوطًا سِىٓءَ بِهِمْ وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا وَقَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ ۖ إِنَّا مُنَجُّوكَ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَكَ كَانَتْ مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٣٣)
```

```english
And when Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed on their account and in a predicament for their sake. But they said, ‘Do not be afraid, nor grieve! We shall deliver you and your family, except your wife: she will be one of those who remain behind. (33)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا مُنزِلُونَ عَلَىٰٓ أَهْلِ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ رِجْزًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْسُقُونَ (٣٤)
```

```english
We are indeed going to bring down upon the people of this town a punishment from the sky because of the transgressions they used to commit.’ (34)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَد تَّرَكْنَا مِنْهَآ ءَايَةًۢ بَيِّنَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ (٣٥)
```

```english
Certainly We have left of it a manifest sign for people who exercise their reason. (35)
```

```arabic
وَإِلَىٰ مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا فَقَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱرْجُوا۟ ٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ (٣٦)
```

```english
And to Midian We sent Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah, and expect [to encounter] the Last Day, and do not act wickedly on the earth causing corruption.’ (36)
```

```arabic
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ فِى دَارِهِمْ جَـٰثِمِينَ (٣٧)
```

```english
But they impugned him, whereupon the earthquake seized them, and they lay lifeless prostrate in their homes. (37)
```

```arabic
وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَا۟ وَقَد تَّبَيَّنَ لَكُم مِّن مَّسَـٰكِنِهِمْ ۖ وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ وَكَانُوا۟ مُسْتَبْصِرِينَ (٣٨)
```

```english
And ‘Ad and Thamud, [whose fate] is evident to you from their habitations. Satan made their deeds seem decorous to them, thus he barred them from the way [of Allah], though they used to be perceptive. (38)
```

```arabic
وَقَـٰرُونَ وَفِرْعَوْنَ وَهَـٰمَـٰنَ ۖ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مُّوسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ سَـٰبِقِينَ (٣٩)
```

```english
And Korah, Pharaoh, and Haman. Certainly, Moses brought them manifest proofs, but they acted arrogantly in the land; though they could not outmaneuver [Allah]. (39)
```

```arabic
فَكُلًّا أَخَذْنَا بِذَنۢبِهِۦ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِ حَاصِبًا وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ أَخَذَتْهُ ٱلصَّيْحَةُ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ خَسَفْنَا بِهِ ٱلْأَرْضَ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ أَغْرَقْنَا ۚ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٤٠)
```

```english
So We seized each [of them] for his sin: among them were those upon whom We unleashed a rain of stones, and among them were those who were seized by the Cry, and among them were those whom We caused the earth to swallow, and among them were those whom We drowned. It was not Allah who wronged them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves. (40)
```

## (Commentary)

When the Glorified mentioned at the beginning of the chapter that trial (الفتنة) — *the divine system of testing and tribulation* — is a divine sunnah (سنة إلهية) from which there is no deviation, and that it had occurred in previous nations, He followed that by referring to the stories of seven of the past prophets and their nations. They are: Nuh, Ibrahim, Lut, Shu‘ayb, Hud, Salih, and Musa (peace be upon him). Allah tried and tested them; thus, those among them who were saved were saved, and those who perished perished. The Glorified mentioned both salvation and destruction in the first three stories, while in the last four, He mentioned only destruction.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا نُوحًا إِلَى قَوْمِهِ فَلَبِثَ فِيهِمْ أَلْفَ سَنَةٍ إِلَّا خَمْسِينَ عَامًا فَأَخَذَهُمُ الطُّوفَانُ وَهُمْ ظَالِمُونَ** — "Certainly We sent Noah to his people, and he remained among them for a thousand-years-less-fifty. Then the flood overtook them while they were wrongdoers." (29:14). In *al-Majma‘*: The flood (الطوفان) is abundant, overwhelming water, so called because it "circles" (يطوف) the regions of the earth due to its vastness. It is also said: It is anything that surrounds a thing with abundance and intensity, whether it be a torrent, wind, or darkness, though it is most commonly used for a flood of water.

The expression "a thousand years less fifty" (ألف سنة إلا خمسين عاماً) rather than saying "nine hundred and fifty years" is for emphasis of the great length (التكثير). The verse is apparent in that the "thousand less fifty" was the duration of Nuh’s (peace be upon him) mission (الدعوة) from his appointment until the flood took them. This differs from what is in the current Torah, which claims it was the duration of his entire life (عمره). Reference to this has already been made in his stories in the commentary on Surah Hud, and the rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَأَنْجَيْنَاهُ وَأَصْحَابَ السَّفِينَةِ وَجَعَلْنَاهَا آيَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ** — "Then We delivered him and the occupants of the Ark, and made it a sign for all the worlds." (29:15). That is, We saved Nuh and the companions of the Ark who rode with him, who were his family and a small number of believers, and they were not among the wrongdoers.

His saying: **وَجَعَلْنَاهَا آيَةً لِلْعَالَمِينَ** — "and made it a sign for all the worlds." It is apparent that the pronoun refers to the event (الواقعة) or the salvation (النجاة). As for it referring back to the Ark (السفينة), it is somewhat far-fetched. All the worlds (العالمين) refers to the many diverse groups of subsequent generations.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ ذَلِكُمْ خَيْرٌ لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ** — "And Abraham, when he said to his people, ‘Worship Allah and be wary of Him. That is better for you, should you know." (29:16). This is conjoined to His saying: **نُوحًا** (Nuh), meaning: And We sent Ibrahim to his people.

His saying to his people: **اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ** — "Worship Allah and be wary of Him" is a call to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and a warning, as evidenced by the subsequent verses; thus, the sentence conveys the sense of exclusivity (الحصر).

Furthermore, the idolaters (الوثنية) do not worship Allah, the Glorified; they only worship others based on their claim that He, the Exalted, cannot be worshipped except through the effective causes in the world that are near to Him, such as the angels and the jinn. Had He been worshipped, He would be worshipped alone without any partner. Thus, calling them to the worship of Allah by saying: **اعْبُدُوا اللهَ** (Worship Allah) implies calling to Him alone, even if it is not restricted by a particle of exclusivity.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا وَتَخْلُقُونَ إِفْكًا** — "What you worship instead of Allah are only idols, and you invent a lie..." (29:17) to the end of the verse. Idols (الأوثان) is the plural of *wathan* (وثن), which is a statue (الصنم). Falsehood (الإفك) is a matter turned away from its proper face, whether in speech or action.

His saying: **إِنَّمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا** — "What you worship instead of Allah are only idols" is an exposition of the futility of idol worship, through which it becomes clear that the worship of Allah is the true worship; in short, the restriction of true worship to Him, the Exalted. The word "idols" (أوثاناً) is indefinite (منكر) to indicate the weakness of their status and that their divinity is a mere claim with no reality behind it. That is: You worship nothing besides Allah except idols whose state is such-and-such.

Therefore, He followed the sentence with His saying: **وَتَخْلُقُونَ إِفْكًا** — "and you invent a lie," meaning you fabricate a falsehood by naming them gods and then worshipping them. Thus, there is a God whose worship is obligatory, but He is Allah, the One, and not the idols.

His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ لَا يَمْلِكُونَ لَكُمْ رِزْقًا** — "Indeed those whom you worship besides Allah have no control over your provision." (29:17). This is a justification (تعليل) for what was mentioned regarding their fabrication of lies by naming idols as gods and worshipping them. The gist is that those whom you worship besides Allah—the idols, insofar as they are images of the "near ones" among the angels and jinn—you only worship them to gain benefit, which is that they might be pleased with you and thus provide for you and bestow sustenance upon you. However, they do not possess any provision for you.

For Allah is the One who possesses your provision (الرزق), which is the cause that sustains your existence, because He is the One who created you and created your provision, making it the sustainer of your survival. Ownership (الملك) follows creation (الخلق) and origination (الإيجاد).

Therefore, He followed it with His saying: **فَابْتَغُوا عِنْدَ اللهِ الرِّزْقَ وَاعْبُدُوهُ وَاشْكُرُوا لَهُ** — "So seek [your] provision from Allah, and worship Him and thank Him." (29:17). That is: Seek provision from Allah because He is the One who possesses it; so do not worship them, but worship Allah and be grateful to Him for what He has provided you and the various blessings He has bestowed upon you. It is obligatory to thank the Benefactor (المنعم) for what He has bestowed.

His saying: **إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ** — "To Him you shall be brought back" (29:17) is in the position of justification for His saying: **وَاعْبُدُوهُ وَاشْكُرُوا لَهُ** (and worship Him and thank Him). For this reason, it was brought as a separate clause without a conjunction. In this justification, He turns them away from worshipping the Deity for the sake of seeking provision toward worshipping Him for the sake of the Return (الرجوع) and the Reckoning (الحساب). For were it not for the Resurrection (المعاد), there would be no realized cause for worshipping the Deity, since provision and its like have specific cosmic causes (أسباب كونية) other than acts of worship and devotions, and it does not increase or decrease by faith or disbelief. However, the felicity of the Day of Reckoning differs based on faith, disbelief, worship, and gratitude. Therefore, let the return to Allah be the motive for worship and gratitude, rather than the seeking of provision.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِنْ تُكَذِّبُوا فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أُمَمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَا عَلَى الرَّسُولِ إِلَّا الْبَلَاغُ الْمُبِينُ** — "And if you impugn [me], then nations before you have impugned [their apostles], and the Apostle’s duty is only to communicate in clear terms." (29:18). It is apparent that this is part of the speech of Ibrahim (peace be upon him). Some have mentioned that it is a discourse from Him, the Exalted, to the polytheists of the Quraysh, but this is not without some far-fetchedness.

The meaning of the condition and its result at the beginning of the verse is that impugnment (التكذيب) is what is expected from you, for it is like a prevailing sunnah among the polytheistic nations; nations before you have impugned, and you are of them and among the last of them. And there is nothing upon me, in my capacity as a messenger, except the clear communication (البلاغ المبين).

It is possible that the meaning is that your state in your impugnment is like the state of the nations before you; their impugnment did not benefit them at all when the punishment of Allah befell them, and they could not escape in the earth or in the heaven, nor did they have any guardian or helper besides Allah. So shall it be with you. His saying: **وَمَا عَلَى الرَّسُولِ** (and the Apostle’s duty is only...) is suitable for both interpretations.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا كَيْفَ يُبْدِئُ اللهُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ إِنَّ ذَلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ** — "Have they not seen how Allah originates the creation and then repeats it? That is indeed easy for Allah." (29:19). This verse, up to the completion of five verses, is from the speech of Him, the Exalted, occurring within the story to establish the proof for the Resurrection (المعاد) — *the return of the soul to the body for judgment* — and to remove their deeming it improbable. It is connected to what preceded insofar as the primary reason for their impugning the messengers was their denial of the Resurrection, as indicated by Ibrahim’s saying: **إِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ وَإِنْ تُكَذِّبُوا فَقَدْ كَذَّبَ أُمَمٌ مِنْ قَبْلِكُمْ** (To Him you shall be brought back. And if you impugn...).

So His saying: **أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا** (Have they not seen...) etc., the pronoun refers to the impugners among all nations, past and future. The "seeing" (الرؤية) intended here is intellectual perception (النظر العلمي) rather than visual sight. His saying: **كَيْفَ يُبْدِئُ اللهُ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ** (how Allah originates the creation and then repeats it) is in the position of the object for His saying: **يَرَوْا** (they see), with "repeats it" (يعيده) conjoined to the position of "originates" (يبدئ), contrary to those who see it conjoined to "Have they not seen." The interrogative is for rebuke (التوبيخ).

The meaning is: Have they not known the modality of origination and then repetition? That is, they are of the same genus, which is the bringing into existence of that which was not. His saying: **إِنَّ ذَلِكَ عَلَى اللهِ يَسِيرٌ** — "That is indeed easy for Allah." The reference here is to the repetition after the origination. In this is the removal of the sense of improbability, because it is an origination after an origination. Since absolute power (القدرة المطلقة) relates to bringing into existence (الإيجاد), it is permissible for it to relate to origination after origination. In reality, it is a transfer of creation from one abode to another, and the settling of the travelers toward Him in the Abode of Permanence (دار القرار).

The saying of some that the meaning of origination and then repetition is the origination of creation and then the creation of their likes after their annihilation is not sound, because it does not suit the argumentation for the Resurrection, which is the return of the very thing that perished, not its like.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ بَدَأَ الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ اللهُ يُنْشِئُ النَّشْأَةَ الْآخِرَةَ إِنَّ اللهَ عَلَى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** — "Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how He has originated the creation.’ Then Allah will bring about the next genesis. Indeed Allah has power over all things." (29:20). This verse, up to the completion of three verses, is a command to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to address them with that which completes the proof against them. He guides them to travel through the earth to observe how He began the creation and originated them despite their differing natures, varying colors, and forms, without a prior model or restriction or limit in their number or preparation. In this is a proof of the lack of limitation in divine power; thus, He brings about the next genesis (النشأة الآخرة) just as He originated the first genesis. The verse is similar in meaning to His saying: **وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولَى فَلَوْلَا تَذَكَّرُونَ** — "Certainly you have known the first genesis, then why do you not take admonition?" (56:62).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَرْحَمُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُقْلَبُونَ** — "He punishes whomever He wishes and has mercy on whomever He wishes, and to Him you will be returned." (29:21). This is part of the commanded speech. It is apparent that it is an exposition of His saying: **يُنْشِئُ النَّشْأَةَ الْآخِرَةَ** (He will bring about the next genesis). To "turn" (قلب) a thing is to transform it from its face or state, such as making its bottom its top or making its interior its exterior. This latter meaning suits His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرَائِرُ** — "on the day when the secrets are examined." (86:9).

They have interpreted "turning" (القلب) as returning (الرد). It is said in *al-Majma‘*: *al-Qalb* is the return and the sending back; so its meaning is that you are returned to the state of life in the Hereafter where no one possesses benefit or harm except Allah. This is a subtle meaning by which the sense of returning to Allah and being sent back to Him is explained: it is their standing in a position where causes (الأسباب) are severed from them and none rules therein except Allah, the Glorified. Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying: **وَرُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلَاهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ مَا كَانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ** — "and they will be returned to Allah, their true Master, and what they used to fabricate will forsake them." (10:30).

The gist of the meaning is: The next genesis is a genesis in which Allah punishes whom He wills—who are the criminals—and has mercy on whom He wills—who are others—and to Him you are returned, so none other than Him shall rule over you.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي السَّمَاءِ وَمَا لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ** — "You cannot frustrate [Him] in the earth or in the sky, nor do you have any guardian or helper besides Allah." (29:22). This is part of the commanded speech and a description of their state on the Day of Resurrection, just as the previous verse was a description of His state, the Exalted, on that day.

So His saying: **وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِي السَّمَاءِ** — "You cannot frustrate [Him] in the earth or in the sky" means that you are not able to frustrate Him, the Exalted, on that day by escaping Him or exiting from His judgment and authority through flight, or by leaving His kingdom and penetrating the regions of the earth and the sky. Thus, the verse follows the course of His saying: **يَا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ إِنِ اسْتَطَعْتُمْ أَنْ تَنْفُذُوا مِنْ أَقْطَارِ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ فَانْفُذُوا** — "O company of jinn and humans! If you can pass through the confines of the heavens and the earth, then do pass!" (55:33).

It is said: The speech is in the sense of "those in the sky," so "those" was omitted because the context indicates it, and the estimation is: "You cannot frustrate [Him] in the earth, nor can those in the sky frustrate [Him] in the sky."

This is far-fetched, and the context's indication of it is not conceded. Even if it were based on this, it would suffice that the discourse is for the generality of creation by giving preponderance (تغليب) to the human side addressed over others like the jinn and angels. The meaning would be: "You, O assembly of creation, cannot frustrate [Him] in the earth or in the sky."

His saying: **وَمَا لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ** — "nor do you have any guardian or helper besides Allah." That is: You have no guardian (ولي) today besides Allah to take charge of your affairs and suffice you against Allah, nor any helper (نصير) to assist you, strengthen your side, and complete your deficient power so as to grant you victory over Him, the Glorified.

The verse—as you see—negates their prevailing over Allah or frustrating Him by exiting and refusing His judgment in all its forms. They do not independently achieve this, which is His saying: **وَمَا أَنْتُمْ بِمُعْجِزِينَ** (You cannot frustrate...), nor do others independently achieve it, which is His saying: **وَمَا لَكُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ** (nor do you have... any guardian), nor does the collective of them and others frustrate Him, the Exalted, which is His saying: **وَلَا نَصِيرٍ** (nor any helper).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِآيَاتِ اللهِ وَلِقَائِهِ أُولَئِكَ يَئِسُوا مِنْ رَحْمَتِي وَأُولَئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ** — "As for those who deny the signs of Allah and the encounter with Him, they have despaired of My mercy, and for such there is a painful punishment." (29:23). This is a discourse directed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), outside of the previous commanded speech "Say, 'Travel over the land...'" etc. What is sought therein is to inform him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) of the explicit truth regarding who shall be wretched and perish on the Day of Resurrection, for He had left that ambiguous in His first saying: **يُعَذِّبُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَرْحَمُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ** (He punishes whomever He wishes and has mercy on whomever He wishes).

Evidence for this is the address in "those" (أُولَئِكَ) twice; had it been from the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), it would have been said: "those of you" (أولئكم).

This is also supported by His saying: **مِنْ رَحْمَتِي** (of My mercy). For the transition from something like our saying: "Those have despaired of the mercy of Allah" or "of His mercy" in the third-person context as required by the situation, to His saying: **أُولَئِكَ يَئِسُوا مِنْ رَحْمَتِي** (they have despaired of My mercy) conveys confirmation and acknowledgment in addition to the original report. Thus, it provides an explicit designation of the people of punishment. This is further supported by the repetition of the demonstrative pronoun and the emphasis in the context.

In specifying the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) with this report, there was a strengthening of his noble soul and a sidelining of them from being fit to hear the like of it, as they do not believe.

The meaning of "the signs of Allah" (آيات الله)—based on the absolute nature of the term—is all the proofs indicating Unity (الوحدانية), Prophethood (النبوة), and the Resurrection (المعاد), including cosmic signs, prophetic miracles, and among them the Quran. Thus, disbelief in the signs of Allah includes, by its generality, disbelief in the Resurrection. Mentioning disbelief in the "encounter" (اللقاء), which is the Resurrection, after disbelief in the signs is the mention of the specific after the general. The reason for this is to indicate the importance of belief in the Resurrection, since with the denial of the Resurrection, the matter of the true religion becomes futile from its foundation, as is apparent.

The meaning of "mercy" (الرحمة) is that which contrasts with punishment and is inseparable from Paradise (الجنة). The application of "mercy" to Paradise has been repeated in His speech by way of necessity, such as His saying: **فَأَمَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ** — "As for those who have faith and do righteous deeds, their Lord will admit them into His mercy." (45:30) and His saying: **يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشَاءُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ وَالظَّالِمِينَ أَعَدَّ لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا** — "He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy, and for the wrongdoers He has prepared a painful punishment." (76:31).

The meaning of attributing "despair" (اليأس) to them is either their actual engagement in it, for by their denial of the afterlife, they are despairing of eternal felicity and the everlasting Paradise; or it is a metaphor (كناية) for His final decree that no disbeliever shall enter Paradise.

The meaning is: Those who denied the signs of Allah indicating the true religion, and especially the Resurrection, those have despaired of mercy and Paradise, and for those is a painful punishment.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قَالُوا اقْتُلُوهُ أَوْ حَرِّقُوهُ فَأَنْجَاهُ اللهُ مِنَ النَّارِ** — "But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Kill him, or burn him!’ Then Allah delivered him from the fire..." (29:24). This is a derivation from His saying at the beginning of the story: **وَإِبْرَاهِيمَ إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاتَّقُوهُ** (And Abraham, when he said to his people, ‘Worship Allah and be wary of Him’).

The appearance of His saying: **قَالُوا اقْتُلُوهُ أَوْ حَرِّقُوهُ** (they said, ‘Kill him, or burn him!’) is that each side of the alternative was the saying of a group among them. What was meant by killing was killing with the sword and its like; this was their saying when they first conspired to punish him, even if they later agreed upon burning him, as He said: **قَالُوا حَرِّقُوهُ وَانْصُرُوا آلِهَتَكُمْ** — "They said, ‘Burn him, and help your gods...’" (21:68). It is also possible that the alternative came from all of them due to their initial hesitation regarding his matter, followed by their agreement on burning him.

His saying: **فأَنْجَاهُ اللهُ مِنَ النَّارِ** — "Then Allah delivered him from the fire" contains an omission and brevity (إيجاز). Its estimation is: Then they agreed to burn him, so they kindled a fire and threw him into it, and Allah saved him from it. The story has been detailed in various places in His speech.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقَالَ إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا مَوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا** — "He said, ‘You have taken idols [for worship] besides Allah for the sake of affection between you in the life of the world..." (29:25) to the end of the verse. Since they had no intellectual proof for taking idols, nothing remained for them to follow as a tradition (سنة) except the following of the tradition of those they aggrandize and respect, such as fathers for sons, honored leaders for their followers, friends for their friends, and ultimately, the nation for its individuals. This connecting cause is the mainstay of what preserves national traditions, keeping them practiced and established.

Following the tradition of idolatry is, in reality, one of the effects of social death; the common folk see it from one another, and national affection (المودة القومية) prompts them to imitate and follow the tradition like them. Then, this very following preserves national affection and maintains unity and agreement.

This is the state of the commoners among them. As for the elite, they might rely on what they consider a proof, though it is no proof, such as their saying that Allah, the Glorified, is too majestic to be encompassed by sense, imagination, or intellect, so our devotional orientation cannot relate to Him. Thus, it is obligatory that we draw near to some of those who have favor with Him, like the angels and jinn, so they may bring us closer to Him and intercede for us with Him.

So His saying: **إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا مَوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا** — "You have taken idols [for worship] besides Allah for the sake of affection between you in the life of the world" is a discourse from him (peace be upon him) to the generality of his people regarding their taking of idols for national affection, to rectify thereby the affairs of their social worldly life. They had answered him with this when he asked them about their state: **إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِ مَا هَذِهِ التَّمَاثِيلُ الَّتِي أَنْتُمْ لَهَا عَاكِفُونَ قَالُوا وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا لَهَا عَابِدِينَ** — "When he said to his father and his people, ‘What are these images to which you keep clinging?’ They said, ‘We found our fathers worshipping them.’" (21:52-53); **قَالَ هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ إِذْ تَدْعُونَ أَوْ يَنْفَعُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَضُرُّونَ قَالُوا بَلْ وَجَدْنَا آبَاءَنَا كَذَلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ** — "He said, ‘Do they hear you when you call [them]? Or do they benefit you, or harm?’ They said, ‘No, but we found our fathers doing likewise.’" (26:72-74).

From here it becomes clear that His saying: **مَوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ** (affection between you) is suitable to be in the accusative case by the removal of the preposition (منصوباً بنزع الخافض) with the estimation of the "lam" of justification (لام التعليل); affection, on this view, is a cause leading to the taking of idols. It is also suitable to be an object of purpose (مفعولاً له), where affection is the intended goal of taking idols. However, the end of the verse only aligns with the second view, as will become clear.

Then he (peace be upon him) followed His saying: **إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُمْ** (You have only taken...) etc., with His saying: **ثُمَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكْفُرُ بَعْضُكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ وَيَلْعَنُ بَعْضُكُمْ بَعْضًا** — "Then on the Day of Resurrection you will disown one another and curse one another" (29:25). He explains to them the consequence of taking idols for affection, which is the inner reality of this intended affection that will appear on the day when secrets are examined. For they sought this meager enjoyment through polytheism (الشرك), which is the greatest injustice and the largest of the destructive major sins; they gathered upon it and agreed, but the reality of their deed will appear to them, and its evil consequence will catch up with them. Thus, they will disassociate from one another and deny one another.

What is meant by "some of you disbelieving in others" (يكفر بعضكم ببعض) is their gods disowning them and disassociating from them, as He, the Exalted, said: **سَيَكْفُرُونَ بِعِبَادَتِهِمْ وَيَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا** — "They will soon deny their worship and they will be their adversaries." (19:82) and He said: **وَيَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ** — "and on the Day of Resurrection they will deny your polytheism." (35:14). In the same sense is the disassociation of the followed from their followers, as He, the Exalted, said: **إِذْ تَبَرَّأَ الَّذِينَ اتُّبِعُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوا وَرَأَوُا الْعَذَابَ وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبَابُ** — "When those who were followed will disown those who followed [them], and they will sight the punishment and all their means will be cut off." (2:166). What is meant by "some of you cursing others" is each group cursing its companion; He, the Exalted, said: **كُلَّمَا دَخَلَتْ أُمَّةٌ لَعَنَتْ أُخْتَهَا** — "Every time a nation enters [hell], it will curse its sister [nation]." (7:38).

Then He followed that with His saying: **وَمَأْوَاكُمُ النَّارُ وَمَا لَكُمْ مِنْ نَاصِرِينَ** — "and your abode will be the Fire, and you will have no helpers." (29:25). This is an indication of the arrival of the evil consequence and the occurrence of the recompense, which is the Fire wherein is eternal destruction, and there is no helper to assist them or repel the punishment from them. They only sought affection to help, cooperate, and support one another in life, but it turned into enmity and opposition on the Day of Resurrection, inheriting disassociation and abandonment.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَآمَنَ لَهُ لُوطٌ وَقَالَ إِنِّي مُهَاجِرٌ إِلَى رَبِّي إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "Thereupon Lot believed in him, and he said, ‘I am indeed migrating toward my Lord. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-wise." (29:26). That is, Lut believed in him. Faith (الإيمان) is made transitive with the "lam" just as it is with the "ba," and the meaning is the same.

His saying: **وَقَالَ إِنِّي مُهَاجِرٌ إِلَى رَبِّي** — "and he said, ‘I am indeed migrating toward my Lord." It is said the pronoun refers to Lut, and it is also said it refers to Ibrahim. The latter is supported by His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Ibrahim: **وَقَالَ إِنِّي ذَاهِبٌ إِلَى رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ** — "He said, ‘I am going toward my Lord; He will guide me.’" (37:99).

It is as if the meaning of migrating to Allah is leaving his homeland and departing from among his polytheistic people to a land where the polytheists would not oppose him or prevent him from worshipping his Lord. Thus, counting the migration as being "toward Allah" is a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي).

His saying: **إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-wise." That is, the Mighty (العزيز) who does not let those He helps be humiliated, the Wise (الحكيم) who does not let those He protects be lost.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَجَعَلْنَا فِي ذُرِّيَّتِهِ النُّبُوَّةَ وَالْكِتَابَ** — "And We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and We ordained prophethood and the Book among his descendants..." (29:27). Its meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَآتَيْنَاهُ أَجْرَهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** — "and We gave him his reward in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous." (29:27). Reward (الأجر) is the recompense that corresponds to an action and returns to its doer. The difference between it and "wage" (الأجرة) is that wage is specific to worldly recompense, while reward (الأجر) encompasses both this world and the Hereafter. The difference between it and "recompense" (الجزاء) is that reward is only used for what is good and beneficial, while recompense encompasses good and evil, beneficial and harmful.

The most common usage in His speech is the use of the word "reward" (الأجر) for the recompense of devotional action that Allah, the Glorified, has prepared for His believing servants in the Hereafter, such as stations of proximity (مقامات القرب), degrees of guardianship (درجات الولاية), and among them Paradise. Yes, it has occurred in His saying, the Exalted, narrating from Yusuf (peace be upon him): **إِنَّهُ مَنْ يَتَّقِ وَيَصْبِرْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "Indeed if one is God-wary and patient, Allah does not waste the reward of the virtuous." (12:90) and His saying: **وَكَذَلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ يَشَاءُ نُصِيبُ بِرَحْمَتِنَا مَنْ نَشَاءُ وَلَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "Thus We established Joseph in the land, to settle in it wherever he wished. We dispense Our mercy on whomever We wish, and We do not waste the reward of the virtuous." (12:56), applying "reward" to good worldly recompense.

So His saying: **وَآتَيْنَاهُ أَجْرَهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا** — "and We gave him his reward in this world" could mean the giving of a good worldly reward. In this case, it is more appropriate that "in this world" (في الدنيا) be connected to "reward" rather than to "giving." This meaning is perhaps supported by His saying, the Exalted, regarding him (peace be upon him) in another place: **وَآتَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةً وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** — "We gave him good in this world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous." (16:122). For it is apparent that what is meant by "good" (حسنة) is a good life or a good livelihood, and its "giving" is the actual bestowing of it, not just its decree and writing.

It is also possible that what is meant is the advancement of what is prepared for the generality of believers in the Hereafter—namely, the stations of proximity—in his favor (peace be upon him), and giving him that in this world. An enumeration of what the Quran mentions of his stations (peace be upon him) has already preceded in his stories in the commentary on Surah al-An‘am.

His saying: **وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** — "and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous." Discussion on this has preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدِ اصْطَفَيْنَاهُ فِي الدُّنْيَا وَإِنَّهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ لَمِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** — "Certainly We chose him in the present world, and in the Hereafter he will indeed be among the Righteous." (2:130) in the first volume of the book.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ مَا سَبَقَكُمْ بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْعَالَمِينَ** — "And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘You indeed commit an indecency none in the world has ever committed before you." (29:28). That is: And We sent Lut, or: And remember Lut when he said to his people. His saying: **إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ** — "You indeed commit an indecency" is a report given by way of amazement and denunciation. What is meant by "indecency" (الفاحشة) is the approaching of males.

His saying: **مَا سَبَقَكُمْ بِهَا مِنْ أَحَدٍ مِنَ الْعَالَمِينَ** — "none in the world has ever committed before you" is an initial sentence (استئناف) that clarifies the meaning of the indecency and emphasizes it. It is as if the meaning is that this act had not spread in any nation before them to this extent; or the sentence is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the subject of "you commit" (لتأتون).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَالَ وَتَقْطَعُونَ السَّبِيلَ وَتَأْتُونَ فِي نَادِيكُمُ الْمُنْكَرَ** — "Do you indeed come to men, and cut off the way, and commit abominations in your gatherings?..." (29:29) to the end of the verse. This is an interrogation regarding a matter that a listener should not believe and a person of intellect should not accept; hence it was emphasized with the "nun" and the "lam." This context testifies that what is meant by "coming to men" is sodomy, and by "cutting off the way" is the neglect and cancellation of the path of procreation, which is coming to women. Thus, "cutting off the way" is a metaphor for turning away from women and abandoning marriage with them. By "committing abominations in your gatherings"—and the gathering (النادي) is the assembly where they meet, and it is not called *nadiyan* unless its people are in it—is meant committing the indecency or its hideous preliminaries in the sight of the group.

It is said: What is meant by "cutting off the way" is cutting off the path of those passing through their lands, for they used to perform this act upon those passing through their territory. They would throw stones at the wayfarer; whoever was hit became the "property" of the thrower, so they would take his wealth, perform the act with him, and fine him three dirhams. They had a judge who would rule thus. It is also said: Rather, they used to commit highway robbery. You have seen that the context dictates otherwise.

It is said: What is meant by "committing abominations in the gathering" is that their assemblies included various types of abominations and hideous acts, such as cursing, obscenity, gambling, throwing stones at passersby, playing stringed instruments and flutes, exposing private parts, sodomy, and the like. You have seen what the context requires.

His saying: **فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قَالُوا ائْتِنَا بِعَذَابِ اللهِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ** — "But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Bring us Allah’s punishment, if you are truthful.’" (29:29). This was mockery and derision from them. It appears from their answer that he was warning them of Allah's punishment. Allah said in his story in another place: **وَلَقَدْ أَنْذَرَهُمْ بَطْشَتَنَا فَتَمَارَوْا بِالنُّذُرِ** — "He had already warned them of Our punishment, but they disputed the warnings." (54:36).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قَالَ رَبِّ انْصُرْنِي عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْمُفْسِدِينَ** — "He said, ‘My Lord! Help me against the corrupt people.’" (29:30). This is a request for victory and a prayer from him against them. He counted them as corrupt (المفسدين) because of their action which corrupts the earth, cuts off progeny, and threatens humanity with extinction.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَمَّا جَاءَتْ رُسُلُنَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ بِالْبُشْرَى قَالُوا إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوا أَهْلِ هَذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ إِنَّ أَهْلَهَا كَانُوا ظَالِمِينَ** — "When Our messengers brought Abraham the good news, they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town. Its people are indeed wrongdoers.’" (29:31). This is a summary of the story of the destruction of Lut's people. That was through messengers from the angels whom Allah sent first to Ibrahim (peace be upon him); they gave him and his wife the good news of Ishaq and Ya‘qub, then informed him that they were sent to destroy the people of Lut. The story is detailed in Surah Hud and elsewhere.

His saying: **قَالُوا إِنَّا مُهْلِكُوا أَهْلِ هَذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ** — "they said, ‘We are indeed going to destroy the people of this town." That is, they said to Ibrahim. In using the near demonstrative—"this town" (هذه القرية)—there is an indication of its proximity to the land where Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was staying, which was the Holy Land.

His saying: **إِنَّ أَهْلَهَا كَانُوا ظَالِمِينَ** — "Its people are indeed wrongdoers." This is a justification for their destruction on the grounds that they were wrongdoers in whom the vice of injustice had become settled. The apparent requirement was to say: "Indeed they were wrongdoers," but the explicit noun was placed instead of the pronoun to indicate that their injustice was a specific injustice belonging to them that necessitated destruction, and not just the general injustice that people were afflicted with at that time. It is as if it were said: "Indeed its people, insofar as they are its people, are wrongdoers."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قَالَ إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا قَالُوا نَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ فِيهَا لَنُنَجِّيَنَّهُ وَأَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ كَانَتْ مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ** — "He said, ‘Lot is in it.’ They said, ‘We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind.’" (29:32). The appearance of the context is that he (peace be upon him) intended by his saying: **إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا** (Lot is in it) to avert the punishment because Lut was in it and the destruction of its people would include him. So they answered him that they were not unaware of that; rather, there were others with him who would not be included in the punishment, namely his family, except his wife.

However, he (peace be upon him) was not ignorant of the fact that Allah, the Glorified, would not punish Lut while he was a sent prophet, even if the punishment included everyone else in his town; nor was he fearing for him or being terrified by His power over them. Rather, he (peace be upon him) intended by his saying: **إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا** to avert the punishment from the people of the town out of honor for Lut, not to repel it from Lut himself. So he was answered that they were commanded to save him and take him out from among the people of the town, along with his family, except his wife who was among those remaining behind.

The proof for what we have mentioned is His saying, the Exalted, in Surah Hud at this point in the story: **فَلَمَّا ذَهَبَ عَنْ إِبْرَاهِيمَ الرَّوْعُ وَجَاءَتْهُ الْبُشْرَى يُجَادِلُنَا فِي قَوْمِ لُوطٍ إِنَّ إِبْرَاهِيمَ لَحَلِيمٌ أَوَّاهٌ مُنِيبٌ يَا إِبْرَاهِيمُ أَعْرِضْ عَنْ هَذَا إِنَّهُ قَدْ جَاءَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ وَإِنَّهُمْ آتِيهِمْ عَذَابٌ غَيْرُ مَرْدُودٍ** — "So when the awe had left Abraham and the good news had reached him, he pleaded with Us for the people of Lot. Abraham was indeed most forbearing, plaintive, [and] penitent. ‘O Abraham, let this matter alone! Your Lord’s edict has certainly come, and an irreversible punishment shall indeed overtake them.’" (11:74-76). These verses are as clear as can be that Ibrahim (peace be upon him) was defending the people of Lut, not Lut himself.

Thus, the appearance of his speech (peace be upon him) in the verse we are currently discussing is a defense of Lut, and the messengers followed him in that, keeping his speech on its apparent meaning and answering that they were not ignorant of that; they knew better who was in it and were aware that Lut was in it along with his family who should not be punished, but they would save him and his family except his wife. However, what Ibrahim (peace be upon him) intended by his speech was to repel the punishment from the people of the town, so he was answered that it was a settled matter, as indicated by the verses of Surah Hud.

The commentators have long disputes regarding His saying: **إِنَّ أَهْلَهَا كَانُوا ظَالِمِينَ** (Its people are indeed wrongdoers) and His saying: **قَالَ إِنَّ فِيهَا لُوطًا** (He said, ‘Lot is in it’), which we have refrained from addressing due to lack of benefit. Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the lengthy works.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَمَّا أَنْ جَاءَتْ رُسُلُنَا لُوطًا سِيءَ بِهِمْ وَضَاقَ بِهِمْ ذَرْعًا وَقَالُوا لَا تَخَفْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ** — "When Our messengers came to Lot, he was distressed on their account and felt helpless for them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid, nor grieve..." (29:33) to the end of the verse. The two plural pronouns in "distressed on their account" (سيء بهم) and "helpless for them" (ضاق بهم) refer to the messengers, and the "ba" is for causality. That is, distress—which is a bad state—took hold of him because of them, and his capacity was constricted because of them, as they were in the form of handsome, beardless youths for whom he feared from the people. Then the people intended evil toward them, and Lut was too weak to repel them from them while they were guests staying in his house.

His saying: **وَقَالُوا لَا تَخَفْ وَلَا تَحْزَنْ** — "They said, ‘Do not be afraid, nor grieve." That is: There is no potential danger threatening you, nor any certain one befalling you. For fear (الخوف) is only regarding a possible disliked thing, and grief (الحزن) is regarding a disliked thing that has occurred.

His saying: **إِنَّا مُنَجُّوكَ وَأَهْلَكَ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَكَ كَانَتْ مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ** — "We will surely deliver you and your family, except your wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind." (29:33). That is, those remaining in the punishment. This is a justification for the negation of fear and grief.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنَّا مُنْزِلُونَ عَلَى أَهْلِ هَذِهِ الْقَرْيَةِ رِجْزًا مِنَ السَّمَاءِ بِمَا كَانُوا يَفْسُقُونَ** — "We are indeed going to bring down a punishment from the sky on the people of this town because of the transgressions they used to commit." (29:34). This is an exposition of the punishment indicated by His saying: **إِنَّا مُنَجُّوكَ وَأَهْلَكَ** (We will surely deliver you and your family). *al-Rijz* (الرجز) is punishment.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ تَرَكْنَا مِنْهَا آيَةً بَيِّنَةً لِقَوْمٍ يَعْقِلُونَ** — "Certainly We have left of it a manifest sign for a people who exercise their reason." (29:35). The feminine pronoun refers to the town. "Leaving" (الترك) is preservation. That is: We have preserved from the town a clear sign for a people who exercise their reason, so they may take heed and be wary of Allah. These are the remaining ruins of it after its destruction by the descent of punishment.

Today, its location is unknown and no trace of it remains. It is sometimes said that water overwhelmed it later and it is the Sea of Lut (the Dead Sea). However, the verse is apparent—as you see—that it was visible and known at the time of the Quran's revelation. Even clearer than this is His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنَّهَا لَبِسَبِيلٍ مُقِيمٍ** — "Indeed it is on a standing highway." (15:76) and His saying: **وَإِنَّكُمْ لَتَمُرُّونَ عَلَيْهِمْ مُصْبِحِينَ وَبِاللَّيْلِ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ** — "Indeed you pass by them at dawn and at night. Do you not exercise your reason?" (37:137-138).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِلَى مَدْيَنَ أَخَاهُمْ شُعَيْبًا فَقَالَ يَا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَارْجُوا الْيَوْمَ الْآخِرَ وَلَا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ** — "And to Midian [We sent] Shu‘ayb, their brother. He said, ‘O my people! Worship Allah and expect the Last Day, and do not act wickedly on the earth, causing corruption." (29:36). He calls them to the worship of Allah, which is monotheism, and to the expectation of the Last Day, which is belief in the Resurrection, and that they should not cause corruption in the earth. The primary form of their corruption therein—as mentioned in their story in other places—was the deficiency in the balance and the measure.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الرَّجْفَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دَارِهِمْ جَاثِمِينَ** — "But they impugned him. So the earthquake overtook them, and they lay lifeless in their homes." (29:37). The earthquake (الرجفة) is intense agitation, as mentioned by al-Raghib. *al-Jathm* and *al-Juthum* (الجثوم) in a place is sitting in it or kneeling on the ground, which is a metaphor for death. The meaning is: They impugned Shu‘ayb, so the intense agitation or the intense earthquake overtook them, and they became dead in their home, motionless.

He said in their story in another place: **وَأَخَذَتِ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ فَأَصْبَحُوا فِي دِيَارِهِمْ جَاثِمِينَ** — "and the Cry overtook those who were wrongdoers, and they lay lifeless in their homes." (11:94). It is inferred from this that they were destroyed by both the Cry (الصيحة) and the Earthquake (الرجفة).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَ وَقَدْ تَبَيَّنَ لَكُمْ مِنْ مَسَاكِنِهِمْ** — "And ‘Ad and Thamud, [whose fate] is indeed evident to you from their dwellings..." (29:38) to the end of the verse. He changed the context for stylistic variation (تفنناً), so He began by mentioning ‘Ad and Thamud. Likewise, in the following verse, He began by mentioning Qarun, Pharaoh, and Haman, unlike the stories of the nations mentioned previously where He began by mentioning their prophets, such as Nuh, Ibrahim, Lut, and Shu‘ayb. His saying: **وَعَادًا وَثَمُودَ** (‘Ad and Thamud) are in the accusative case due to an estimated verb, the estimation being: "And remember ‘Ad and Thamud."

His saying: **وَزَيَّنَ لَهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ أَعْمَالَهُمْ فَصَدَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّبِيلِ وَكَانُوا مُسْتَبْصِرِينَ** — "Satan made their deeds seem glamorous to them and barred them from the way, though they used to be perceptive." (29:38). Satan's making their deeds seem glamorous to them is a borrowed metaphor (كناية استعارية) for making their evil deeds beloved to them and strengthening their attachment to them. His barring them from the way is turning them away from the way of Allah, which is the way of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*. For this reason, some have said that the meaning of their being "perceptive" (مستبصرين) is that they were previously upon the simple primordial nature.

However, it is apparent—as preceded in the commentary on His saying: **كَانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ** — "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets..." (2:213)—that the era of the simple primordial nature was before the mission of Nuh (peace be upon him), and ‘Ad and Thamud were after Nuh. Thus, their being "perceptive" before being barred from the way means they were living upon the worship of Allah and the religion of monotheism, which is the religion of the primordial nature.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقَارُونَ وَفِرْعَوْنَ وَهَامَانَ وَلَقَدْ جَاءَهُمْ مُوسَى بِالْبَيِّنَاتِ فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَمَا كَانُوا سَابِقِينَ** — "And Korah, Pharaoh, and Haman. Certainly Moses brought them manifest proofs, but they acted arrogantly in the land; yet they could not outrun [Our punishment]." (29:39). "Outrunning" (السبق) is a borrowed metaphor for prevailing (الغلبة). The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَكُلًّا أَخَذْنَا بِذَنْبِهِ** — "So We seized each [of them] for his sin..." (29:40) to the end of the verse. That is, each of the mentioned nations We seized for its sin. Then He began the detail, saying: **فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِ حَاصِبًا** — "Among them were those upon whom We unleashed a killer storm." (29:40). *al-Hasib* (الحاصب) refers to stones, and it is said: the wind that throws pebbles. According to the first, they are the people of Lut; according to the second, the people of ‘Ad. **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ أَخَذَتْهُ الصَّيْحَةُ** — "and among them were those who were overtaken by the Cry," and they are the people of Thamud and the people of Shu‘ayb. **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ خَسَفْنَا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ** — "and among them were those whom We caused the earth to swallow," and he is Qarun. **وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ أَغْرَقْنَا** — "and among them were those whom We drowned," and they are the people of Nuh, Pharaoh, Haman, and their people.

Then the Glorified returned to all the mentioned stories and the end to which the matter of those nations led—seizure and punishment. He explained with a general exposition that what befell them was not due to any injustice from Him, the Glorified, but rather due to their own injustice to themselves. He said: **وَمَا كَانَ اللهُ لِيَظْلِمَهُمْ وَلَكِنْ كَانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ** — "Allah would not wrong them, but it was they who used to wrong themselves." (29:40). That is, Allah recompenses them for their injustice because the abode is an abode of trial and testing, which is the divine sunnah from which there is no deviation. So whoever is guided is guided for his own soul, and whoever goes astray, it is against it.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abu ‘Amr al-Zubayri from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): In a hadith in which he mentions the meanings of disbelief (الكفر), he said: "The fifth aspect of disbelief is the disbelief of disassociation (كفر البراءة). Allah, the Exalted, said: **وَقَالَ إِنَّمَا اتَّخَذْتُمْ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ أَوْثَانًا مَوَدَّةَ بَيْنِكُمْ فِي الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا ثُمَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ يَكْفُرُ بَعْضُكُمْ بِبَعْضٍ وَيَلْعَنُ بَعْضُكُمْ بَعْضًا** — meaning some of you shall disassociate from others." — the hadith.

**I say**: This meaning was also narrated in *al-Tawhid* from ‘Ali (peace be upon him) in a long hadith in which he answers what he was asked regarding the [apparent] inconsistency of verses. Therein: "And disbelief (الكفر) in this verse is disassociation (البراءة); He says: some of them shall disassociate from others. Its parallel in Surah Ibrahim is the saying of Satan: **إِنِّي كَفَرْتُ بِمَا أَشْرَكْتُمُونِ مِنْ قَبْلُ** — 'I disown your having pleaded me as a partner [with Allah] before' (14:22), and the saying of Ibrahim, the Friend of the All-Beneficent: **كَفَرْنَا بِكُمْ** — 'We disown you' (60:4), meaning: we disassociate from you."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Jabir: That the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) forbade *al-khadhf* (throwing pebbles), and it is the saying of Allah: **وَتَأْتُونَ فِي نَادِيكُمُ الْمُنْكَرَ** — "and commit abominations in your gatherings."

**I say**: This meaning was also narrated from several authors of the *Jawami‘* from Umm Hani bint Abi Talib. The wording of the hadith is: She said: "I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the saying of Allah: **وَتَأْتُونَ فِي نَادِيكُمُ الْمُنْكَرَ**. He said: 'They used to sit by the road, throw pebbles at the wayfarer, and mock them.'"

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Abu Zayd al-Hammad from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): In a hadith regarding the descent of the angels upon Ibrahim with the good news, he said: "Ibrahim said to them: 'Why have you come?' They said: 'For the destruction of the people of Lut.' He said to them: 'If there are a hundred believers in it, will you destroy them?' Jibra’il said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are fifty?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are thirty?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are twenty?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are ten?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there are five?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'If there is one?' He said: 'No.' He said: 'Indeed Lut is in it.' They said: 'We know better who is in it. We will surely deliver him and his family, except his wife: she shall be one of those who remain behind.'"

Al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (peace be upon him) said: "I do not know this saying except that he was trying to spare them, and it is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **يُجَادِلُنَا فِي قَوْمِ لُوطٍ** — 'he pleaded with Us for the people of Lot.'"
